Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe flip through some materials when I remembered or when something was due soon. Six hours a week sounds about right, but it was scattered—like twenty minutes here, an hour there. I'm more of a visual person so I'd watch videos when I could, but organizing a proper schedule just never happened for me.
Q2: It varied a lot depending on what was coming up. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then cram a bit when a deadline was approaching. Regular study times weren't really my thing—I'd fit it in around my extracurriculars and whatever else was going on. The pattern was pretty chaotic if I'm being honest.
Q3: Usually it was because an assignment was due or I got a notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, then log right back out. It wasn't like I was excited to be there—more like a chore I had to do occasionally. Deadlines were really the main driver.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried at the beginning of the semester to make it a habit, but it never stuck. I'd log in maybe twice a week at most, and even then it felt forced. It never became natural or automatic for me—always felt like something I had to remind myself to do.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I learn better visually. I watched quite a bit of video content because I could actually absorb information that way. Discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better than the lectures. But honestly, even the useful stuff felt like a lot of effort.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and any text-heavy resources. My eyes would just glaze over trying to get through dense material. I'd delay looking at anything that seemed complicated or time-consuming. If it wasn't visual or didn't seem immediately necessary for an assignment, it probably got skipped.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to an assignment or exam. If it was visual and didn't take forever, even better. But honestly, my motivation was so low that even useful activities felt like a drag. I needed things to be quick and obviously relevant or I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it—simple as that. Required stuff at least had consequences attached, which gave me some reason to engage. Optional things just felt like extra work I didn't have the energy for, even if they might have helped.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to watch later on my phone or laptop without being connected to the LMS. I also used YouTube sometimes to find explanations of concepts that weren't clicking. Some of my studying happened through notes I took during class that I'd review on paper.
Q10: There were times I'd be going over my handwritten notes or rewatching downloaded videos, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also talk through problems with friends from my extracurricular activities who were in similar courses. That kind of informal studying was real, but the system wouldn't know about it.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download a video or PDF once, then never go back to the LMS for it. I preferred having stuff saved locally so I could access it whenever without logging in. So my actual engagement with the material was probably higher than my login frequency would suggest.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My anxiety was through the roof even though I wasn't feeling stressed in the traditional sense—it's weird, I know. I'd feel this underlying dread about the course but couldn't motivate myself to actually do anything about it. The CBT sessions helped my mood, but the engagement was already pretty low by then.
Q13: Definitely. I had extracurriculars taking up time, and when I was confused about course material, I'd just avoid it rather than push through. Fatigue was a big one too—I'd be too tired to really focus on anything academic. All of that meant I used the LMS less and less as the semester went on.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the video playing but be on my phone or thinking about something else entirely. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. It was like going through the motions without actually learning anything.
Q15: I postponed exam prep constantly even though I knew it was killing my grade. There was this anxiety around it that made me avoid it—like if I didn't look at it, maybe it wouldn't be as bad. I knew it was important, but the avoidance felt easier in the moment. Classic self-sabotage, I guess.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd see a due date coming up and finally kick into gear, usually at the last minute. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have logged in at all. They created this pressure that temporarily overrode my lack of motivation.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. I'd see comments on assignments and think 'okay, that's good to know' but not actually adjust my approach. My assignment scores were decent enough that I figured I was doing fine, which probably made me complacent. The exam was a wake-up call I got too late.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, but it didn't really push me to engage more consistently. Weekly modules meant I could ignore things until they were almost due. If there had been more frequent check-ins or something, maybe I would've stayed more on track. The flexibility kind of enabled my drifting.
Q19: The video content made things easier since that's how I learn best. But the amount of reading and text-based resources made engagement harder for me. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced sometimes—I'd post just to meet the quota without really caring about the conversation. More visual and interactive elements would've helped.
Q20: I think sometimes people are trying to figure things out but struggling to actually complete work. Maybe they're anxious about submitting something imperfect, or they're confused and keep revisiting materials without making progress. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels.
Q21: That kind of describes me in a way. I didn't log in much, but I did okay on assignments because I'd work offline and just submit when needed. My exam score tanked everything though. So low LMS activity can still mean decent assignment work, but it might hide gaps that show up on exams.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the mental effort. They're like a shadow of engagement, not the real thing.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in consistent effort. It's not just showing up—it's being mentally present and wanting to understand the material. By that definition, I wasn't really engaged this semester. I was just surviving, doing the minimum to get by.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety or how much I was avoiding things out of fear rather than laziness. It wouldn't capture the offline studying I did or the conversations with friends that actually helped me understand stuff. And it definitely wouldn't show that despite failing, the CBT sessions actually helped me feel better about myself overall.
